learning_objectives: Classify digital media into categories: text, image, audio, video, and interactive
Explain the technical characteristics that define each media type
Identify appropriate media types for different communication objectives
readings: Lister et al., New Media: A Critical Introduction, Ch. 1-2
Nielsen Norman Group, 'Content Types and Formats for Digital Media'

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital Media Fundamentals builds on your DC101 foundation by giving you hands-on competence with the media formats that digital communicators use daily. By the end of this course, you will be able to produce competent work in text, image, audio, and video for digital platforms.
key_concepts: Media taxonomy: text, static image, motion graphics, audio, video, interactive
File formats and their trade-offs: compression vs. quality, compatibility vs. features
Resolution, aspect ratio, and platform specifications for major channels
Accessibility requirements for each media type: alt text, captions, transcripts
The production pipeline: planning, creation, editing, optimization, distribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examining the same message delivered as a blog post, an infographic, a podcast clip, a short video, and an interactive web story, analyzing the strengths and constraints of each format
setup_requirements: Free Adobe Creative Cloud student access or Canva Pro trial

review.value: Lossy compression (JPEG, MP3) permanently discards data to reduce file size; lossless compression (PNG, FLAC) reduces size without data loss, important for choosing format based on quality needs and distribution constraints
The proportional relationship between width and height of a visual or video asset; different platforms require different ratios (16:9 for YouTube, 9:16 for Stories, 1:1 for Instagram feed)

class_schedule.topic: Visual Design Principles for Digital Media
learning_objectives: Apply the principles of visual hierarchy, contrast, alignment, and proximity to digital layouts
Select appropriate typography and color palettes for digital communications
Critique visual designs using established design principles
readings: Williams, The Non-Designer's Design Book, Ch. 1-4
Google Material Design guidelines, 'Color and Typography' sections

lecture_notes.introduction: You do not need to be a graphic designer to create effective digital communications, but you do need to understand the visual principles that make content scannable, attractive, and professional. These principles apply to everything from social media graphics to email newsletters to presentations.
key_concepts: Visual hierarchy: guiding the eye through content using size, color, contrast, and position
The four CRAP principles: Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, Proximity
Typography for screens: sans-serif defaults, readability at various sizes, font pairing
Color theory basics: primary, secondary, complementary; brand color systems
White space as a design element: giving content room to breathe
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning a poorly laid out email newsletter step by step, applying each design principle and discussing the improvement at each stage
setup_requirements: Access to Google Fonts for typography exploration

review.value: Contrast (make different things noticeably different), Repetition (reuse visual elements for consistency), Alignment (connect elements visually through deliberate positioning), Proximity (group related items together)
The arrangement of elements in order of importance, guiding the viewer's eye from the most critical information to supporting details through size, color, contrast, and spatial positioning

learning_objectives: Apply basic composition rules (rule of thirds, leading lines, framing) to digital photography
Perform essential image editing operations: cropping, exposure correction, color balance, and resizing
Optimize images for web delivery by balancing quality and file size
readings: Peterson, Understanding Exposure, Ch. 1-2 (adapted for smartphone photography)

lecture_notes.introduction: Strong visuals are the foundation of digital engagement. Most digital communicators will not work with professional photographers for daily content. Learning to take and edit good photographs with accessible tools is an essential skill.
key_concepts: Composition fundamentals: rule of thirds, leading lines, symmetry, framing
Lighting for smartphone photography: natural light, window light, avoiding mixed lighting
Image editing workflow: import, crop, exposure, white balance, sharpening, export
Web optimization: choosing JPEG vs. PNG vs. WebP, appropriate resolution for web use
Batch processing: editing multiple images efficiently for social media content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live photo shoot and edit session: taking three product photos with a smartphone, then editing them in a free tool (Snapseed or Canva) for Instagram post quality
setup_requirements: Snapseed or similar free photo editing app installed
Small objects for product photography practice

review.value: Dividing the frame into a 3x3 grid and placing key subjects along the gridlines or at intersections, creating more dynamic compositions than centering
A modern image format developed by Google that provides superior compression for both lossy and lossless images, reducing file size 25-35% compared to JPEG at equivalent quality

learning_objectives: Record clear audio using accessible equipment and proper microphone technique
Perform basic audio editing: noise reduction, volume normalization, trimming, and export
Explain the role of audio in digital communications beyond podcasting
readings: Rabiger, Directing the Documentary, Ch. 21 - Sound

lecture_notes.introduction: Audio is often the most overlooked media skill for digital communicators, yet it underpins podcasts, video voiceovers, social media audio, and even voice-based interfaces. Good audio production starts with understanding recording fundamentals.
key_concepts: Microphone types and placement: dynamic vs. condenser, distance and angle
Recording environment: reducing echo, eliminating background noise, treating a room cheaply
Audio editing workflow: import, noise reduction, EQ basics, compression, normalization, export
File formats for audio: WAV for editing, MP3 for distribution, AAC for Apple platforms
Audio in non-podcast contexts: social media voiceovers, corporate video narration, IVR systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Recording a 60-second voiceover using a USB microphone and Audacity, then editing it to broadcast quality: noise removal, EQ, compression, and final export
setup_requirements: USB microphone or smartphone earbuds with microphone
Quiet recording space (or tips for improvising one)

review.value: The baseline level of unwanted sound in a recording environment; reducing the noise floor through room treatment and microphone technique is more effective than removing noise in post-production
Adjusting the overall level of an audio recording so that the peak or average loudness reaches a target standard, ensuring consistent volume across different content pieces

learning_objectives: Plan a short video using a shot list and basic storyboard
Apply fundamental camera techniques: framing, stability, and basic camera movements
Edit a short video with cuts, transitions, titles, and exported for social platforms
readings: Stockman, How to Shoot Video That Doesn't Suck, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Video is the dominant content format on nearly every digital platform. Fortunately, the barrier to entry has never been lower. A smartphone, natural light, and free editing software are enough to create professional-quality short-form video content.
key_concepts: Pre-production essentials: shot list, storyboard, script or talking points
Camera fundamentals for smartphones: stabilization, exposure lock, focus lock, grid overlay
Framing for social video: vertical for Stories/Reels/TikTok, horizontal for YouTube, square for feed posts
Basic editing workflow: import, assembly cut, rough cut, fine cut, titles, audio, color, export
Platform-specific export settings: resolution, frame rate, bitrate for Instagram, YouTube, LinkedIn
lecture_notes.demonstration: Shooting and editing a 30-second promotional video for a campus event using only a smartphone and CapCut (free editing app), from shot planning to final export
setup_requirements: CapCut, iMovie, or DaVinci Resolve installed
Tripod or improvised stabilization (stack of books)

review.value: A pre-production document listing every camera setup needed for a video, including shot type (wide, medium, close-up), subject, action, and any notes on framing or movement
The first rough edit where all usable clips are placed in sequence order without fine-tuning timing, transitions, or audio; it serves as the structural foundation of the final video

learning_objectives: Adapt a single content piece across text, image, audio, and video formats
Evaluate which media type best serves a given communication objective
Create a multi-format content package from a single source story
readings: Pulizzi, Content Inc., Ch. 8 - Repurposing Content
Buffer, 'The Complete Guide to Repurposing Content' (blog post)

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective digital communicators do not create each piece of content from scratch. They start with a core idea and adapt it across formats and platforms, maximizing the return on their creative investment while respecting each platform's unique strengths.
key_concepts: Content atomization: breaking a large content piece into smaller platform-specific assets
Format-first thinking: starting with the platform and its constraints rather than forcing content into an unfamiliar format
Adaptation vs. repurposing: transforming content for a new format vs. simply reposting in the same format
The content multiplication framework: one long-form piece becoming 10+ short-form assets
Quality standards across formats: maintaining brand voice and visual consistency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Taking a single 800-word blog post and live-adapting it into an Instagram carousel, a 60-second video script, a Twitter thread, an audio teaser, and an email newsletter blurb
setup_requirements: Sample blog post provided on LMS

review.value: The practice of breaking a single substantial content piece (article, report, video) into numerous smaller, platform-optimized assets, each capable of standing alone while linking back to the original
Content designed for a specific platform's format, audience behavior, and algorithmic preferences rather than cross-posted identically across all channels

quizzes.topic: Digital Media Types and Visual Design

questions.question: Explain the difference between lossy and lossless compression. For a digital communications professional creating images for a website, when would you choose JPEG (lossy) over PNG (lossless), and vice versa?
questions.answer: Lossy compression permanently removes data to reduce file size (JPEG), while lossless compression reduces size without losing any data (PNG). Choose JPEG for photographs and complex images with many colors where slight quality loss is acceptable and small file size is important for page load speed. Choose PNG for graphics with text, logos, screenshots, or any image requiring transparency, where sharp edges and exact color reproduction matter more than file size.

rubric.key: Correctly defines lossy and lossless compression
Identifies appropriate use cases for JPEG
Identifies appropriate use cases for PNG

questions.question: List the four CRAP design principles and explain how applying Proximity would improve a cluttered social media infographic.
questions.answer: The four CRAP principles are Contrast (making different elements noticeably different), Repetition (reusing visual elements for consistency), Alignment (connecting elements through deliberate positioning), and Proximity (grouping related items together). Applying Proximity to a cluttered infographic would improve it by grouping related data points, labels, and icons close together while separating unrelated sections with whitespace. This creates visual chunks that the viewer can process one at a time, reducing cognitive load and making the infographic scannable rather than overwhelming.

rubric.key: Applies Proximity to infographic with specific improvements

questions.question: Describe three steps you would take to improve audio quality when recording a voiceover in a non-professional environment (such as a dorm room or home office).
questions.answer: Three steps: (1) Reduce room echo by recording in a small, furnished space with soft surfaces (closets work well), or draping blankets over hard surfaces near the microphone. (2) Minimize background noise by turning off HVAC, closing windows, and recording during quiet hours. (3) Use proper microphone technique by positioning the microphone 6-8 inches from the mouth, slightly off-axis to reduce plosives, and using a pop filter or windscreen if available.

questions.question: What is a shot list, and why is it more useful than a storyboard for a solo digital communicator producing a 60-second social media video?
questions.answer: A shot list is a pre-production document listing every camera setup needed for a video, including shot type, subject, action, and framing notes. It is more useful than a storyboard for a solo digital communicator because it is faster to create (no drawing required), functions as a production checklist that prevents forgetting shots during a solo shoot, and focuses on practical execution details rather than visual composition, which matters more when one person is handling all production roles simultaneously.

rubric.key: Explains why it suits solo production

major_assignments.title: Multi-Format Content Package
major_assignments.description: Create a complete multi-format content package for a real or fictional organization, starting from a single core story and adapting it into five distinct media formats: a written blog post, a social media graphic series, an audio clip, a short video, and an email newsletter. Each piece must be platform-optimized and production-ready.
objectives: Demonstrate competency in text, image, audio, and video production
Adapt a single narrative across formats while maintaining brand consistency
Optimize each content piece for its target platform's specifications
Apply visual design, audio production, and video editing skills from the course
deliverables: Written blog post (600-800 words) with embedded media
Social media graphic series (3-5 images) formatted for Instagram or LinkedIn
Audio clip (60-90 seconds) suitable for podcast teaser or social audio
Short video (30-60 seconds) formatted for Instagram Reels or TikTok
Email newsletter layout incorporating elements from all other formats

criteria: Core story is compelling and clearly adapted across all formats
Each piece stands alone while contributing to the overall narrative
Writing is clear, engaging, and free of errors
Brand voice is consistent across all formats
Images meet platform specifications and apply visual design principles
Audio is clean, properly leveled, and professionally edited
Video uses appropriate framing, stability, and editing techniques
All files are exported in correct formats with appropriate quality settings
Each piece is formatted for its target platform's requirements
Aspect ratios, durations, and file sizes are platform-appropriate
Accessibility features are included (captions, alt text, transcripts)
Cohesive multi-format package demonstrating cross-media storytelling
Strong technical execution across all media types
Platform-optimized content respecting each channel's requirements
Professional quality suitable for a real organizational portfolio

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on media production concepts and techniques

lab_exercises.description: Weekly hands-on production exercises in image, audio, and video

major_assignment.description: Multi-format content package demonstrating cross-media production skills

midterm_portfolio.description: Portfolio of best production work from the first half of the course

final_portfolio.description: Cumulative portfolio demonstrating growth and proficiency across all media types

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused lab absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction. Lecture attendance is expected.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Production exercises submitted more than 3 days late receive no credit. Extensions require advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed collaboratively, but all graded submissions must be individual work. Sharing raw project files is prohibited; sharing techniques and advice is encouraged.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All media assets must be original or properly licensed. Stock assets must be credited. Submitting work produced outside this course or by AI generation tools without disclosure is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop capable of running video editing software, a smartphone with a camera, headphones, and internet access. Free software options are provided for all exercises. USB microphones are available for checkout from the media lab.
